probe_designs.content: CelebA's 40 attributes are highly imbalanced (e.g. Bald ~2%, Wearing_Necktie ~7%, Smiling ~48%). With limit_train_batches=100 (~6,400 random images/epoch), rare positives may be near-absent, making macro-F1 dominated by attributes the model never learns. Probe: compute positive prevalence per attribute over the actual sampled subset vs. the full CelebA prior, and report which attributes have <50 positive examples per epoch. Healthy: subset prevalence tracks full-dataset prevalence and every attribute sees enough positives; unhealthy: rare attributes have zero/near-zero positives, indicating macro-F1 is being computed on under-trained labels.
possible_sources: Liu et al., 'Deep Learning Face Attributes in the Wild' (ICCV 2015, CelebA)
He et al., 'Learning from Imbalanced Data' (IEEE TKDE 2009)
torchmetrics multilabel documentation

probe_designs.content: Metrics threshold the sigmoid at a fixed 0.5, but BCE-optimal decision thresholds for imbalanced attributes are usually far from 0.5. Probe: sweep per-attribute thresholds and compare macro-F1 at 0.5 against F1 at per-attribute optimal thresholds (tuned on val). Also inspect predicted-probability histograms per attribute. Healthy: 0.5 is near-optimal and probabilities are well spread; unhealthy: large F1 gain from re-thresholding and probability mass collapsed near 0 for rare attributes, signaling the 0.5 metric understates true discriminative ability.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Lipton et al., 'Optimal Thresholding of Classifiers to Maximize F1 Measure' (ECML 2014)

probe_designs.probe_name: Attribute accuracy disparity across the Male subgroup
probe_designs.content: CelebA is widely used to audit demographic bias; 'Male' acts as a protected-attribute proxy. Probe: stratify per-attribute F1/accuracy by the ground-truth Male attribute and compute disparities (e.g. for Smiling, Young, Attractive). Healthy: comparable performance across male/female groups; unhealthy: systematic gaps (e.g. better Smiling detection on one gender) revealing learned spurious correlations and fairness risk.
possible_sources: Buolamwini & Gebru, 'Gender Shades' (FAT* 2018)
Ramaswamy et al., 'Fair Attribute Classification through Latent Space De-biasing' (CVPR 2021)
Denton et al., 'Image Counterfactual Sensitivity Analysis for Detecting Unintended Bias' (2019)

probe_designs.content: Train transforms include RandomVerticalFlip(p=0.4). Faces are not vertically symmetric, so upside-down faces are out-of-distribution and may corrupt orientation-dependent attributes (Eyeglasses, Wearing_Hat, hair). Probe: train/eval with and without vertical flip and measure per-attribute F1 delta; also test invariance by evaluating on vertically flipped val images. Healthy: removing vertical flip leaves metrics stable or improves them; unhealthy: vertical flip measurably degrades spatially-grounded attributes, indicating a harmful augmentation choice.
possible_sources: Shorten & Khoshgoftaar, 'A survey on Image Data Augmentation for Deep Learning' (J Big Data 2019)
timm augmentation recipes (Wightman)

probe_designs.probe_name: Train/val gap under heavy data truncation
probe_designs.content: Only ~6,400 of ~200k images are used per epoch with a pretrained ViT-S and lr=1e-5. Probe: track train vs. val macro-F1/loss curves across epochs and compare against a full-data run on a budget. Healthy: small, stable train-val gap with val/F1 plateauing near reported ~0.76; unhealthy: large gap (overfit to the truncated subset) or val/F1 stuck well below historical baselines, indicating the truncation harms generalization rather than acting as a benign 'probe head'.
possible_sources: Hestness et al., 'Deep Learning Scaling is Predictable, Empirically' (2017)
Dosovitskiy et al., 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words' (ViT, ICLR 2021)

probe_designs.probe_name: BCE loss weighting vs. macro-F1 objective mismatch
probe_designs.content: Loss is unweighted BCEWithLogitsLoss (per-element averaged), which is dominated by majority/easy attributes, while the reported metric is macro-averaged across 40 attributes. Probe: decompose loss contribution per attribute and correlate with per-attribute F1; test pos_weight or focal loss. Healthy: low-loss attributes also have high F1 and the objective tracks macro-F1; unhealthy: rare attributes carry tiny loss yet poor F1, showing the optimization objective is misaligned with the evaluation metric.
possible_sources: Lin et al., 'Focal Loss for Dense Object Detection' (ICCV 2017)
torch.nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss pos_weight documentation
Cui et al., 'Class-Balanced Loss Based on Effective Number of Samples' (CVPR 2019)

probe_designs.content: CelebA attributes are strongly correlated (e.g. Heavy_Makeup↔Wearing_Lipstick↔Male, 5_o_Clock_Shadow↔Male). A model may predict one attribute by leaning on a correlated proxy rather than visual evidence. Probe: compare the model's predicted attribute co-occurrence matrix against the ground-truth label correlation matrix, and test on counterfactual/decorrelated subsets. Healthy: predictions reflect genuine per-attribute signal; unhealthy: predicted correlations exceed label correlations, revealing shortcut reliance that fails on atypical combinations.
possible_sources: Geirhos et al., 'Shortcut Learning in Deep Neural Networks' (Nature MI 2020)
Liu et al., CelebA (ICCV 2015)
Sagawa et al., 'Distributionally Robust Neural Networks' (ICLR 2020)

probe_designs.probe_name: Attention/saliency localization on relevant facial regions
probe_designs.content: For a ViT-S, attribute predictions should attend to the relevant region (Eyeglasses→eye region, Smiling→mouth, Wearing_Hat→top). Probe: use attention rollout or Grad-CAM on validation images and qualitatively/quantitatively check whether high-attention regions overlap the semantically relevant face area. Healthy: attention concentrates on plausible regions; unhealthy: diffuse or background-focused attention (e.g. relying on hair/background for Eyeglasses), suggesting the model learned spurious cues despite decent F1.
possible_sources: Abnar & Zuidema, 'Quantifying Attention Flow in Transformers' (ACL 2020)
Selvaraju et al., 'Grad-CAM' (ICCV 2017)
Chefer et al., 'Transformer Interpretability Beyond Attention Visualization' (CVPR 2021)

probe_designs.content: Inference accepts arbitrary .png/.jpg files (CustomImageDataset), so production inputs differ from the curated CelebA distribution. Probe: evaluate macro-F1 under common corruptions (JPEG compression, blur, brightness/contrast shifts, occlusion) following the ImageNet-C protocol. Healthy: graceful, gradual degradation; unhealthy: sharp F1 collapse under mild corruption, indicating brittleness for real-world inference. Note that ColorJitter in training may help photometric but not blur/occlusion robustness.
possible_sources: Hendrycks & Dietterich, 'Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations' (ICLR 2019)
Bhojanapalli et al., 'Understanding Robustness of Transformers for Image Classification' (ICCV 2021)

probe_designs.probe_name: Pipeline integrity: checkpoint provenance, disabled logging, metric reporting
probe_designs.content: Several integrity risks: ViTsmall.ckpt is external and unverified, Trainer uses logger=False (W&B disabled), and README accuracies (ViT 0.7622) are owner-provided, not computed by this code. Probe: verify the actual loaded weights' source, confirm metrics are computed on a held-out test split (not the truncated val), and reproduce the reported numbers from the saved best-model.ckpt. Healthy: metrics reproducible on test split with documented seed and known checkpoint origin; unhealthy: reported figures cannot be regenerated, or evaluation only ever runs on the 6,400-image truncated val, undermining the stated result.
possible_sources: Pineau et al., 'ML Reproducibility Checklist' (NeurIPS 2019)
PyTorch Lightning Trainer/ModelCheckpoint documentation
Gundersen & Kjensmo, 'State of the Art: Reproducibility in AI' (AAAI 2018)
